data.title: Toro: Pressure From Residential Market; Initiate At Hold
data.description:
Toro (NYSE:TTC) manufactures outdoor products such as turf & landscape maintenance, golf course equipment and snow & ice management. The company is more likely to deliver another year of weak growth in FY25 due to sluggish consumer confidence and a weak housing market. I estimate its stock price is fairly valued; therefore, I am initiating with a hold rating with a fair value of $78 per share.

As shown in the charts below, Toro generates around 78% of revenue from the professional segment, which includes products used for turf maintenance, underground constructions as well as snow and ice management. Their products are used in a variety of end-markets. In addition, Toro sells its outdoor power equipment to residential market through retailers, online and direct-to-consumer channel. Due to the weak housing market and low consumer confidence, its residential business has experienced significant growth pressure, with an 11.4% revenue decline in Q2 FY25.

As depicted in the chart below, Toro has delivered strong growth during the global pandemic period, driven by robust market demands and pricing increases. In the post-Covid era, its business growth has been muted, with only 0.9% and 0.7% growth in FY23 and FY24, respectively. Due to the weak consumer confidence, the company is more likely to deliver another year of weak growth in FY25. Indeed, the company is currently in a business down cycle.

To counter the weak market demands, the company launched its AMP program, a series of cost cutting initiatives to drive margins, as shown in the slide below. As noted in its earnings call, the company has realized $70 million in cost savings so far and they were on track to deliver $100 million in total cost savings by FY27. Notably, they reduced its workforce by 10% in FY25. At the same time, they have optimized its manufacturing footprint and adjusted their product portfolio to save costs. I appreciate the management’s efforts to streamline its overhead costs and improve margins.

Toro reported its Q2 FY25 result on June 5th, posting 2% revenue decline and 1% growth in adjusted EPS, as detailed in the table below:

As discussed previously, the weak residential demands posed significant growth headwinds for the company. Its professional segment grew by 0.8% in revenue during the quarter, with weak shipments of underground and specialty construction products. The management guided for -3% to flat growth for FY25, with an adjusted EPS of $4.15 to $4.30.

Toro has repurchased $200 million of its own shares so far this year. The company has a gross debt leverage of 1.3x, slightly higher than its target range of 1-1.2x. The company has generated a total of $777 million of FCF over the past three years. Considering all these factors, I think Toro has a robust balance sheet to fund its share repurchase and dividend payout.

My key takeaway from the quarter was that the company would continue to cut costs amid weak end-market demands, while also investing in electrifications, autonomous solutions and connected technologies.

For FY25, I anticipate the company’s revenue will decline by 1.8% assuming 1% growth in the professional and 12% decline in residential segment. From FY26 onwards, I predict the company will achieve 5% normalized revenue growth, aligning with the overall market growth.

I anticipate 20bps annual margin expansion from FY26 onwards, driven by 10bps from pricing increase and 10bps from its cost-cutting initiatives. During the earnings call, the management disclosed that tariff duties would be 3% of the total annual cost of goods sold. As such, I calculate its reported operating margin will decline by 80 bps in FY25.

With these assumptions, the DCF can be summarized as follows:

I calculate the FCFF as follows:

I set the WACC to be 9.8% assuming: risk free 4.2%; beta 1.05; equity risk premium 6%; cost of debt 5%; equity $7.63 bn; debt $922 million; tax rate 19%. I assume a 4% terminal growth rate, assuming 2% pricing growth and 2% volume increase. Discounting all the future FCFF and adjusting net debt, the fair value is calculated to be $78 per share, as per my estimate.

Key Risks

For turf maintenance equipment, Toro sustained its market-leader position in the traditional combustion engine market. However, I am not sure if Toro can secure its #1 position in the battery electric motor technology. The situation is quite similar to the automotive industry, where a traditional automaker might not survive in the age of EVs.

Toro also face cost pressure from tariffs on steel and aluminum, which are heavily used in Toro’s products. Other than raising prices, I don’t think the company can find better ways to offset these additional costs.

On August 12th, Toro announced that Edric C. Funk has been appointed President and COO, effective September 1, 2025. While Edric has been with the company for 29 years, the senior management change might cause some near-term disruptions to operations and strategic implementation.

End Note

I favor Toro’s strong market-leading positions in its product categories, and its strategic cost-cutting initiatives. The current weak consumer sentiment has posed significant growth headwinds for the company. I estimate its stock is fairly valued. As such, I am initiating with a hold rating with a fair value of $78 per share.

data.title: Romania And Bulgaria Assume Benchmark Role For Black Sea Wheat Trading
data.description:
By Paul Wightman

At a Glance

  • As the global wheat trade expands, the first futures are trading in Romanian and Bulgarian wheat.
  • Adverse weather in key grain-producing regions is contributing to supply uncertainty.

A new cash-settled futures contract connecting wheat from the export-intensive Black Sea region to the global market is off to a strong start, offering a platform to traders for risk management, arbitrage opportunities with other regional markets and potential cross-hedging strategies.

The Black Sea Wheat (CVB) Financially-Settled (Argus) futures allow for the effective pricing of wheat from one of the biggest exporting regions in the world, particularly from Romania and Bulgaria, which ships wheat from the ports of Constanța, Varna and Burgas (collectively known as CVB). Wheat from Romania and Bulgaria is shipped to destinations across the globe alongside Black Sea wheat supplies from Ukraine and Russia.

A total of around 160,000 metric tons of wheat has been traded in the first month since launch in early June 2025, and the contract is also beginning to trade on a more regular basis. Many traders expect the contract to become the leading international benchmark for the Black Sea region. Additionally, some basis trades across the Black Sea and beyond are expected to be concluded against the CVB wheat futures as the pace of trading develops (see hedging example here).

CVB Wheat, A Reliable Benchmark for Wider Black Sea Trade

The pricing and correlation of CVB wheat to other Black Sea markets make it an effective price-risk management tool for the broader region. Romania and Bulgaria have exported over 56 million tons of wheat since 2020, according to the latest data from Eurostat. Romanian and Bulgarian exports have reached around 13 million tons in the latest U.S. marketing year 2024-2025 (June-May), according to data from the U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA). Export volumes from the CVB ports have increased 16% since 2022.

The daily Argus CVB price is well correlated to the daily Argus price of wheat exports from the Russian Black Sea port of Novorossisk. The stability in overall export levels also helps to make the CVB price a reliable price indicator for the broader Black Sea region. The latest published set of Argus daily prices through June 2025 shows a high correlation between their CVB price Free on Board (FOB) and FOB Novorossisk at 0.97, indicating that the Argus CVB wheat price (which is the basis of the CVB futures) could be readily used as a hedging instrument for cargoes loading out of Novorossisk. Typically, a value of more than 0.80 means that prices are well correlated and that traders can use the price of a commodity to hedge an underlying price exposure to the other market.

Historical Volatility in the Black Sea Wheat Markets Remains Elevated on Continued Supply Uncertainty

The 30-day historical annualized volatility, an important statistical indicator measuring the historical changes in the underlying commodity, shows that CVB wheat has traded in a range of 15% to 30% volatility since September 2023. Higher levels of price volatility typically lead to greater trading interest to manage risk against an underlying commodity.

Compounding the implications of the Russia-Ukraine war are weather-related challenges impacting the 2025 wheat harvest in parts of the Black Sea region. In Russia and Ukraine, there are expected to be smaller crops compared to previous years for the 2025-2026 marketing year. Ukraine is facing its own set of unfavorable weather conditions, with Ukrainian Agriculture Minister Vitaliy Koval estimating that 2025’s grain harvest could fall 10%. Meanwhile, Romania could see its biggest wheat crop since 1997, in part due to more favorable weather conditions. These supply uncertainties have seen heightened levels of volatility in the Black Sea wheat region.

As trading in the Black Sea continues to evolve, the role it could play for pricing on the global stage is likely to increase. Buyers of grain from the Black Sea, such as those in Asia and North Africa, could benchmark cargoes to an increasingly liquid Black Sea reference price. More broadly, as the global wheat trade continues to expand, risk management tools could play an increasingly important role for market participants across the supply chain.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: U.S. IPO Weekly Recap: Bullish And Miami International Complete The Last Major IPOs Of The Summer
data.description:
Eight issuers listed in the US this past week, six of which were eligible for inclusion in our IPO stats, joined by two SPACs. Seven IPOs and eight SPACS submitted initial filings.

Crypto exchange Bullish (BLSH) priced above the upwardly revised range to raise $1.1 billion at a $5.8 billion market cap. The company provides trading and liquidity infrastructure under its “Bullish Exchange” brand, as well as information services under its “CoinDesk” brand, which spans indices, market data, and research reports. Bullish has grown trading volume substantially, and it should benefit from a clearer regulatory environment going forward. The company soared 84% on day one and after some volatile trading, ended the week up 88%.

Financial exchange operator Miami International Holdings (MIAX) priced above the range to raise $345 million at a $2.3 billion market cap. Miami International manages options, equities, and futures exchanges, primarily under the “MIAX” banner. For the six months ended 6/30/25, average daily volume in US options on the MIAX Exchanges was 8.7 million contracts. The company has captured a growing share of the US options industry and has numerous potential new products to drive growth, but it’s heavily exposed to fluctuating trading volume. Miami International ended the week up 35%.

Indonesian travel agency NusaTrip (NUTR) priced at the low end to raise $15 million at a $75 million market cap. NusaTrip states that it is the first Indonesian-based online travel agent (OTA) in Indonesia to receive International Air Transport Association (IATA) accreditation. The company ended the week down 2%.

Israeli biotech Nasus Pharma (NSRX) priced at the low end to raise $10 million at a $74 million market cap. The company is focused primarily on the development of intranasal drugs to treat emergency medical conditions. Nasus ended the week up 1%.

Singapore-based electrical installer Magnitude International (MAGH) priced at the low end to raise $9 million at a $140 million market cap. Under the brand name BNL, the company provides electrical installation for private and public housing, commercial and mixed-use developments, and other types of public facilities. Magnitude ended the week down 63%.

Hong Kong-based event services provider BUUU Group (BUUU) priced at the low end to raise $6 million at a $66 million market cap. The company provides Meetings, Incentives, Conferences, and Exhibitions (MICE) solutions to a variety of clients. BUUU Group ended the week up 3%.

While not included below, Japanese osteopathic health care provider rYojbaba (RYOJ) and Australian biotech Propanc Biopharma (OTCPK:PPCB) also listed in the US this past week, but were too small to be included in our IPO stats.

Week Ahead

There are no IPOs currently scheduled for the week ahead, though some small issuers may still price.

The calendar should stay fairly quiet through the end of the month, with no major launches expected until after Labor Day. The public pipeline doesn’t provide much visibility into fall activity yet, but a few big names appear to be teeing up September listings.

After delaying in the wake of the tariff sell-off, fintech Klarna (KLAR) and ticket marketplace StubHub (STUB) are reportedly planning to go public next month. The companies both updated financials this past week.

A few major issuers also joined the pipeline this past week: crypto exchange Gemini (GEMI), transport-tech Via Transportation (VIA), and systems services provider Legence (LGN).

Street research is expected for seven companies in the week ahead, and three lock-up periods will be expiring.

IPO Market Snapshot

The Renaissance IPO Indices are market cap weighted baskets of newly public companies. As of 8/14/2025, the Renaissance IPO Index was up 14.7% year-to-date, while the S&P 500 was up 10.8%. Renaissance Capital's IPO ETF (IPO) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include Reddit (RDDT) and Astera Labs (ALAB). The Renaissance International IPO Index was up 31.3% year-to-date, while the ACWX was up 22.1%. Renaissance Capital’s International IPO ETF (IPOS) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include Galderma and Horizon Robotics.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 08/16/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

data.title: ASHR: Bullish China Momentum Buttresses The Valuation Case, Eyeing $35
data.description:
It was a busy week of inflation data at home, which makes it easy to overlook what’s happening with wholesale and consumer prices abroad. Key to the US inflation story is China PPI. For many months now, the world’s second-largest economy has been exporting deflation, in a sense. It's month-over-month PPI reading continues to run in the red, which is a welcome trend for the bond market, equity investors, and the doves on the FOMC.

Today, I’m revisiting the Xtrackers Harvest CSI 300 China A-Shares ETF (NYSEARCA:ASHR). I was bullish on the large-cap China fund last October, noting that the price-to-earnings ratio was low while stimulus efforts from Chinese fiscal authorities offered a sanguine backdrop.

ASHR has done well in the past 10 months, returning 7%, though that is about three percentage points of negative alpha to the S&P 500. I reiterate a buy rating.

China PPI Remains Sharply Negative

China's P/E Remains Depressed Both on an Absolute Basis and Relative to History

According to the issuer, ASHR seeks investment results that correspond generally to the performance, before fees and expenses, of the China Securities 300 Index (CSI 300 Index). It offers efficient access to large-cap Chinese stocks.

ASHR is a medium-sized ETF with $2.0 billion in assets under management as of August 14, 2025. That’s down materially from $3.5 billion at the time of my previous assessment. Its annual expense ratio is moderate to high at 65 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is modest at 1.0%.

But share-price momentum has improved markedly since the April low, earning the product a B ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. ASHR is quite risky, however, thanks to highly volatile price action this year and throughout most of its history. Finally, from a liquidity perspective, tradability is high; average daily volume is high at 8 million shares, while the 30-day median bid/ask spread is just three basis points, per DWS.

For an update on the portfolio, the 3-star, Neutral-rated ETF by Morningstar plots on the upper portion of the style box, indicating its significant exposure to Chinese large and mega-caps. From a diversification standpoint, there’s about an even split among value, blend, and growth, but there is no small- or mid-cap access.

On valuation, ASHR remains a compelling buy, in my opinion. Its price-to-earnings ratio is low at just 14.2x, while the long-term EPS growth rate is respectable at 8.3%. The resulting PEG ratio is fair at 1.7x.

ASHR: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

Zooming in on the sector breakdown, key to ASHR’s success is strength in the bank-heavy Financials sector. Resource stocks from the Energy and Materials niches comprise just 11% of the portfolio. Information Technology, meanwhile, is a significant underweight compared to I.T.’s position in the S&P 500 (18% vs 34%).

What I like is that ASHR is not top-heavy, as the largest holding is only 3.9% of the mix.

ASHR: Holdings & Dividend Information

As for seasonality, August has historically been a dreadful month, but ASHR is bucking the long-term trend, which I take as a positive. Performance in September and October has been more mixed, but some bullish vibes have historically come about in November.

ASHR: Navigating Weak Calendar Trends Well Right Now

The Technical Take

With a low valuation, surprising persistent seasonal strength, and a diverse allocation, ASHR’s technical situation is generally healthy, reaffirming the buy call.

Notice in the chart below that shares are right at key resistance at $30. A breakout above the current level would portend a potential upside measured move price objective to slightly above long-term resistance at $35. I think that could be reached by the end of next year if the broad uptrend continues.

Also take a look at the long-term 200-day moving average. It’s below the near-term 50dma with price above both trend indicators, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend. Moreover, the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph ranges in a bullish zone between 40 and 80.

Finally, with a high amount of volume by price now below the current level, there should be ample cushion if we see a downside swing into year-end.

ASHR: Shares At Resistance, Targeting Near $35 In the Quarters Ahead, Support at the 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on ASHR. The valuation case remains solid, while the technicals call for an eventual test of the 2024 peak near $35.

data.title: Cullen/Frost: Back To A Reasonable Price As Costs Continue To Weigh (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Things have been a little mixed for Cullen/Frost (CFR) in recent months. While the earnings of this quality Texan bank are tracking a little better than expected, the market's reward has been a negative 8% return for the shares since my previous update in February - a performance that is around six points worse than the wider regional bank space.

Operational trends are admittedly a little mixed under the hood. The lack of interest rate cuts this year has helped, as has robust credit quality. Revenue is also growing nicely, helped by Frost's ongoing expansion into markets like Austin. On the flip side, the costs associated with this continue to weigh on the bank's efficiency. Even so, Frost is set for over $9.40 per share in net income this year according to current sell-side consensus, which is around 6% higher than expected earlier in the year.

Turning to the shares, having downgraded them to hold on valuation concerns, I am now shifting them back to buy. Frost's valuation multiples have ticked down these past six months due to the discrepancy between its share price performance (down) and earnings and book value (both up). Meanwhile, there is some light at the end of the tunnel in terms of the pay-off from its expansion efforts, which should become more visible as we progress through the back-half of the decade.

Spreads And Credit Look Positive, Costs Less So

Looking back to my last piece, I was a little concerned that interest rate cuts would weigh on Frost this year. On the asset side, Frost's loan book skews more to floating-rate forms of commercial lending, which on the way up was good for a 60% loan beta. With interest rates now 100bps off their peak, Frost has experienced around half of that decline in terms of its down-cycle loan beta. Cash was also running at nearly 20% of the bank's assets when I last covered it.

The liability side of the balance sheet has historically been one of Frost's strongest points, with the firm enjoying one of the clearest funding cost advantages in the space. Frost was paying just 1.6% on its deposits at the peak of the cycle, mapping to a strong ~30% beta. The downside, if that's the right word to use, is that this means there is less opportunity for Frost to cut interest expenses when rates fall. Contributing to that, Frost still has an exceptional 33% of its deposits in non-interest-bearing accounts.

That is the context in which I would view Frost's down-cycle beta. While a 31% pass-through rate might not look great at first blush, it's really just a function of the bank's already-excellent deposit costs.

With the above in mind, the lack of rate cuts so far this year has probably helped. On top of that, deposit costs have fallen modestly, while there's been some movement within the asset mix that will have been a tailwind. For example, higher-yielding loans have inched up 140bps since my last piece to around 44% of overall earning assets. Weighing all that up, Frost's net interest margin is up 14 bps to a strong 3.67%.

A higher interest margin has also been complimented by the bank's ongoing organic growth. Efforts to build up its presence in markets like Dallas and Austin continue, contributing to circa 5% year-on-year growth in earning assets last quarter. Accordingly, quarterly net interest income of $429.6 million was up over 8% year-on-year.

That expansion is also powering growth in the bank's non-interest income, as more customers and deposits mean more fees from adjacent services (charges on accounts, card transaction fees, and so on). Non-interest income came in at $117.3 million last quarter, up over 5% year-on-year.

Credit quality is also a positive right now. Frost booked a 0.2% net charge-off rate over the first two quarters, which is at the low end of initial 2025 guidance. Net losses also remain around 8 bps below Frost's long-run average. Delinquencies aren't rising either. Around 0.3% of the bank's loans were 30-89 days past due at the end of last quarter - a figure that is not only lower than it was at the start of the year, but is also below its late-2010s level (before all the COVID stimulus money).

While I can't predict when there'll be a downturn, credit quality isn't really a general concern I have with this bank. Frost's net charge-off rate has been pretty strong historically, including a very good performance during the 2007-2009 crisis, when net losses peaked below 60 bps. Looking at potential trouble spots, I would note that energy lending is a much smaller part of Frost's loan book these days (around 6% versus 15% a decade ago). Besides, like I said above, loans are only 44% of Frost's earning assets, so credit quality doesn't really have the same degree of impact on its profits as it does at other banks.

Costs arguably remain Frost's weak spot right now, as expenses were up around 9% year-on-year last quarter. Again, that is related to the bank's ongoing branch expansion efforts, and it's basically a case of having to spend money to make money (it takes time for new branches to reach scale, while costs are front-loaded). On the most recent earnings call, management went into some detail on the economics, with each expansion taking around 5 years to hit profitability. Overall, Frost is about breakeven right now on this "new" part of its business.

This is making measures of its efficiency (its costs-to-assets ratio, for example) look worse than they are. Like I said last time, Frost's costs-to-assets ratio is actually higher than it was in the mid-2010s, bucking the trend in the wider space as well as other Texan banks like First Financial (FFIN). As the Dallas and Austin expansions mature over the next 1-2 years, I expect cost leverage to improve, boosting Frost's earnings growth and underlying profitability.

The Valuation

Frost trades for $124.95 as I type, down from $139.02 when I last covered it. Meanwhile, 2025 consensus EPS is up by around 6%, while its book value has risen around 8% to $63.04, aided by lower unrealized losses in the securities book. Accordingly, the stock's price/earnings ratio (based on 2025 consensus) has fallen by ~240bps to 13.25x, while the price/book ratio is down 40bps to 1.98x.

These are very reasonable multiples for this bank. Looking back, Frost has achieved high-single-digit annualized growth in its earnings per share. Book value growth looks worse on the chart, but that's a function of the unrealized losses in its securities portfolio. Strip that out, and annualized growth would also have been in the high single-digit area. Frost also pays a $1 per share quarterly dividend, equal to an ~40% payout ratio and a yield of 3.2%.

Frost's past growth fits nicely with its retained earnings ratio (60%) and return on equity (15.6% over the first half of 2025). Looking ahead, Texas is a relatively high growth state, and Frost only has a ~3.7% share of statewide deposits. I think Frost can gain market share over time. Why? Well, it consistently tops customer satisfaction surveys of the state's banks. I may be guilty of some simplification here, but that's basically what the bank's strategy is - win low-cost deposits from Texan consumers and businesses by offering superior customer service.

With that, I think Frost can carry on compounding at a high-single-digit clip over the long run. And with a 3.2% base yield from the dividend, that gets us to a circa 10%-plus annualized return. I view that as attractive for a bank with low-cost deposits and a track record of conservative underwriting. Accordingly, I am moving the shares back to buy.

data.title: United Bankshares: Credit Quality Still Looking Robust
data.description:
After a rocky start to the year, the past few months have been much better for United Bankshares (NASDAQ:UBSI), with this West Virginian lender returning around 11% since my first piece in March - a performance that basically matches the fortunes of the wider regional bank space.

United's heavy focus on making commercial real estate ('CRE') loans may ordinarily be of concern. However, as I went over last time, underwriting has historically been a strong point for this bank, with Charleston-based United enjoying a meaningfully lower long-term loss rate than the sector.

The above in mind, things have gone about as well as could be expected so far this year. Delinquencies and loan losses both remain relatively benign, while United's earnings have also seen a nice boost from recent M&A. With the shares still trading for a reasonable multiple of tangible book value, I remain bullish.

Earnings Get A Boost From The Latest Piece Of M&A

A serial acquirer, United closed on its most recent purchase back in January, adding around $2 billion in assets to its balance sheet as well as exposure to the Greater Atlanta Area - a new market for the bank.

That deal has also provided a nice boost to recent financial results, with the bank's net interest income rising 22% to $274.5 million. Looking at the drivers of that, the Piedmont deal helped drive 9% year-on-year growth in interest-earning assets. At the same time, accretion from fair value marks was also a tailwind on the margin side, contributing to roughly 31 bps of year-on-year NIM expansion.

While the bank will only see the full benefit from cost synergies next year, expense growth still trailed revenue growth by around 10 points last quarter. That drove United's efficiency ratio around 430bps lower to 48.4%, while its pre-provision net revenue was up around 30% to $158 million. With credit loss provisioning roughly flat year-on-year despite a sizable increase in loans, most of that growth flowed down to the bottom line, with net income up around 25% to $120 million - a new quarterly record for the bank.

Tangible book value per share rose nearly 7% year-on-year to $23.32, benefiting from lower unrealized losses in the securities portfolio.

Credit Still Looking Robust

In terms of competitive advantages, United's management would tout a few things the bank has going for it, including a wider-than-average net interest margin and historically sound credit quality.

I think the latter is the more interesting feature of this bank. On the face of it, there are reasons for concern. United has over 80% of its interest-earning assets in loans for one – a share that's about ten points above the average for banks with over $10 billion in assets. In theory, higher credit loss provisioning would therefore have a greater impact on its underlying profitability.

United's loans skew heavily to commercial real estate, too. Specifically, non-owner occupied CRE and Construction account for around 50% of the book, which is around 20 points higher than the average bank. Likewise, concentration of those categories is over 300% when expressed relative to Tier 1 capital plus the bank's loan loss allowance. Again, well above average.

While these are valid concerns, I will repeat what I said last time - which is that credit losses have typically been an area of outperformance for United. Its long-term average net charge-off rate is around half the peer group average, and this includes a meaningful period of outperformance during the 2007-2009 financial crisis.

Related to that, United appears to enjoy considerable collateral backing. For instance, non-owner occupied Office loans have been a particular area of market concern in recent years, particularly towers in central business districts, as these properties have also had to contend with work-from-home pressures alongside the general headwinds from higher interest rates. United disclosed that the top 60 credits in its non-owner occupied office portfolio had a weighted loan-to-value ratio of 55% based on current loan balances and appraisal values.

Positively, credit quality trends were still looking solid at the halfway point of the year. In terms of losses, United's net charge-off rate was still only 0.14% of total loans over the first two quarters. While that is higher than in recent years, this is off a very low base following the COVID stimulus, with losses basically tracking back to their late-2010s area.

Delinquencies don't appear to be increasing either. Around 0.34% of United's loan book was 30-89 days past due at the end of last quarter. Not only was that down relative to the start of the year, but it's also around 20 bps below the same-period 2019 level. As for the Investor CRE book, only around 0.21% of those loans were similarly delinquent at the end of Q2. That's about the same as its pre-COVID rate.

Finally, there's United's healthy capital levels to consider. While this is a last line of defense, its Common Equity Tier 1 (13.4%) and tangible equity (10.8%) ratios nevertheless look strong.

Credible Path To Double-Digit Returns

As you can see, United hasn't been the best performer over the past decade or so, though it's important to note that multiple compression has played a fairly large role in that. For instance, relative to its current tangible book value, the stock's current quote ($37.64) maps to a multiple of around 1.61x. That's down from roughly 2.58x at this point in 2016.

United's solid profitability should prevent this from being much of a headwind going forward. Last quarter, the bank's bottom line mapped to a strong 14.7% return on average tangible common equity ('ROATCE'). Its ROATCE has averaged a shade over 13% over the past decade. Erring on the side of caution by using the latter figure, I still think United can manage around 6% annualized growth in tangible book value per share, given it pays out around 45% of its earnings via its dividend. The latter is yielding around 3.95%, giving shares a credible path to a 10% annualized return for today's buyer.

Conclusion

United Bankshares has clocked in a pretty good performance since my opening piece back in March. Earnings have risen strongly, its stock has returned over 10%, and credit quality hasn't really shown any signs of deterioration. While these shares haven't been the best performer over the past ten years or so, their current valuation doesn't require aggressive assumptions on growth or profitability to work. On that basis, I remain bullish.

data.title: SandRidge Energy: Promising Early Results From Its Operated Cherokee Development
data.description:
SandRidge Energy (NYSE:SD) reported promising early results from its operated Cherokee development and appears likely to at least end up near the middle of its full year production guidance. It expects its development program to result in 30+% oil production growth by the end of the year compared to Q2 2025 levels.

SandRidge has also made progress with reducing its lease operating costs, with a 21% per BOE reduction compared to Q1 2025, excluding the impact of a one-time accrual adjustment that further reduced its reported LOE. While its LOE will likely be higher in 2H 2025 than Q2 2025 levels, it still is trending to do better than its full year guidance around that.

I've increased my estimate of SandRidge's value slightly (by about 3%) to reflect those positive items. I now estimate its value at around $11.75 to $14.00 per share at long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

Q2 2025 Results

SandRidge reported approximately 17,800 BOEPD (17% oil) in production during Q2 2025. This was roughly the same as its Q1 2025 production. SandRidge dealt with weaker commodity prices during the quarter, resulting in a 20% drop in its realized price per BOE (before hedges) compared to Q1 2025. Including hedges, SandRidge's realized price per BOE went down by 16%.

SandRidge's reported lease operating expenses for Q2 2025 benefited from a one-time $2.1 million non-cash operating accrual adjustment. This accrual dated back to SandRidge's 2016 emergence from bankruptcy.

Without that adjustment, SandRidge's lease operating expenses would have been around $5.35 per BOE in Q2 2025, which is still a 21% reduction from Q1 2025. This was attributed to lower power and workover costs during the quarter.

The lower operating costs helped SandRidge deliver $10 million in free cash flow during the quarter despite weaker commodity prices.

SandRidge also increased its quarterly dividend from $0.11 to $0.12 per share. This adds up to around $18 million in dividends per year based on SandRidge's current share count.

SandRidge repurchased around 0.1 million shares for approximately $1 million during the quarter.

Cherokee Development

SandRidge reported the results of its first operated Cherokee well. This well had an IP-30 of approximately 2,300 BOEPD (49% oil). This is a relatively strong early result that would appear to be in the top 10% of Cherokee wells in SandRidge's data set and should still provide solid returns at current commodity prices.

SandRidge initially mentioned acquiring 22 two-mile locations for its Cherokee inventory. It is drilling eight wells during 2025 (and completing six of them), so that would only leave it with a couple years of remaining inventory at that development pace.

SandRidge thus is looking towards further opportunities to increase its position in the area, although this will also depend on how its remaining Cherokee wells from its 2025 development plans perform.

Notes On Cash Taxes

SandRidge continues to have around $1.6 billion in federal NOL carryforwards. I've never put much direct value in SandRidge's NOLs, other than noting that SandRidge is unlikely to pay cash income taxes at any point in the foreseeable future, even if makes further acquisitions and increases its production.

The tax changes from the budget reconciliation bill don't benefit SandRidge much given its massive amounts of NOLs. Some other companies (that were cash income taxpayers) are seeing significant near-term reductions in their cash taxes as a result of the bill.

2H 2025 Outlook

SandRidge expects its 2025 exit rate to exceed 19,000 BOEPD, with a 30+% increase in its oil production compared to Q2 2025. This suggests an exit rate oil cut of around 20%, with SandRidge ending up near the middle of its full year production guidance.

At current strip prices (roughly $62 to $63 WTI oil and $3.25 NYMEX natural gas) for the second half of 2025, SandRidge is projected to generate $84 million in 2H 2025 revenues after hedges.

Type Barrels/Mcf $ Per Unit $ Million
Oil 660,000 $61.50 $41
NGLs 1,100,000

$16.00

 $18
Natural Gas 9,700,000 $1.95 $19
Interest Income $2
Hedge Value $4
Total Revenue $84

SandRidge's 2025 capex budget is more heavily loaded into the second half of the year, so it may have around $48 million in 2H 2025 capex if it ends up around the middle of its full year expectations.

$ Million
Lease Operating Expense $20
Production Taxes $5
Cash General & Administrative $5
Capital Expenditures $48
Total $78

This would leave it with around $6 million in 2H 2025 free cash flow before approximately $9 million in projected dividends.

SandRidge had $103 million in cash at the end of Q2 2025 and is now projected to end 2025 with around $100 million in cash without any changes to other working capital items (or additional share repurchases).

Notes On Valuation

I previously estimated SandRidge's value at around $11.35 to $13.65 per share based on long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

I've now slightly increased my estimate of SandRidge's value to $11.75 to $14.00 per share based on those same commodity prices.

SandRidge's early Cherokee development results have been positive, although there is only a relatively limited amount of data from that so far. SandRidge has also reduced its lease operating expenses and appears likely to end up near the low-end (or below) of its full-year guidance range, even after factoring out the $2.1 million accrual adjustment.

Those two items result in a slight increase to SandRidge's estimated value.

Conclusion

SandRidge reported positive initial results from its Cherokee development and expects its oil production to increase by over 30% (from Q2 2025 levels) by the end of the year. SandRidge's capex budget in mainly concentrated in the second half of the year, so it is only expected to generate a modest amount of free cash flow during the next two quarters.

SandRidge has also reported strong cost control results, reducing its lease operating expenses to under $6 per BOE in Q2 2025. The cost improvement plus initial Cherokee development success increases my estimate of SandRidge's value slightly to $11.75 to $14.00 per share.

data.title: Dillard's Remains A Problematic Long-Term Play
data.description:
I have been investing for a long time now. While I do have a good track record, I don't have a perfect one. But I do know a dangerous game that investors are playing when I see it. One company that looks attractively priced, and that may be appealing to many investors because of that, is fashion apparel, cosmetics, and home furnishings retailer Dillard's (NYSE:DDS). If you grew up in the US, you have almost certainly been to one of its stores. I remember as a child being dragged around its cavernous aisles, just the same way I was dragged through the aisles of Macy’s (M) and Kohl’s (KSS). Ever since the company was founded in 1938, this has become a common experience for most Americans.

Such an iconic company might be tempting for somebody to buy into, especially when shares are cheap on an absolute basis. And fundamental performance would suggest that the picture for the business is not awful. At least that was the case when management announced financial results for the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year on August 14th. Having said that, the firm continues, on the whole, to lose revenue, profits, and cash flows. And instead of management investing the significant cash flow the company generates into reinventing itself, they prefer to pay it out in the form of distributions and to engage in share buybacks. While this might result in short-term benefits, it jeopardizes the long-term health of the enterprise. And when you consider the nature of this strategy, it's difficult for me to not reaffirm it as a ‘sell’ candidate following my initial ‘sell’ rating on the stock earlier this year.

Not a play for me

Fundamentally speaking, the most recent data provided by management has been decent. Take the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year as an example. Revenue for the business came in at $1.54 billion. That's up from the $1.51 billion reported the previous year. It also translates to a beat over what analysts were expecting of $20 million. This was in spite of the fact that the number of stores the company has in operation dipped from 273 to 272.

The company actually benefited during this time from a 1% increase in comparable store sales because of strength in its juniors’ and childrens’ apparel and ladies’ accessories and lingerie products. The company did report weakness in the home and furniture market. But honestly, that doesn't shock me one bit. After all, the housing market is weak, interest rates are high, and inflationary pressures are rising again. When you add all of these things together, it means that large purchases like furniture would be put off by most consumers.

On the bottom line, management reported earnings per share of $4.66. That's up from the $4.59 per share reported the previous year and it came in $0.66 above what analysts were hoping to see. This translated to a drop, however, in net profits from $74.5 million to $72.8 million. This is because management continues to buy back shares. Year over year, the company reduced its overall share count by 3.7%. The big pain point here seems to have been with the firm's gross profit margin, which fell from 39.1% to 38.1%. Other profitability metrics were largely a disappointment. Yes, operating cash flow went from negative $68.5 million to positive $86.8 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a decrease from $117.4 million to $110.7 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the business contracted from $145.7 million to $137.9 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first half of 2025 compared to the same time 2024. Even though the business reported a sales increase in the second quarter of this year, for the year as a whole so far, revenue is actually down, with comparable store sales flat and the number of locations and operation having declined. It also doesn't help that service charges and other income contracted from $48.5 million to $40.3 million during this time. Net profits and every cash flow metric that I looked at with the exception of operating cash flow all contracted on a year over year basis.

You might recall from my previous article that the company has developed a history of declining revenue, profits, and cash flows. In the chart above, you can see results from the last three fiscal years and in the chart below you can see the overall trend for store count and comparable store sales. These were both taken from my previous article on the retailer. Although the trend is not indicative of a rapid decline like what we saw with other retailers like Sears Holdings and J.C. Penney Company back in the day, it is still disconcerting to say the least.

My big problem with the company has been that management seems more focused on returning capital to shareholders as opposed to reinventing the business for long term success. From 2022 through 2024, management allocated $1.02 billion to dividends. They paid another $839 million out for share buybacks.

Combined, this $1.86 billion is equivalent to 24% of the market value of the company as it stands today. Of course, management has the cash to do this. The firm had a net cash position as of the end of the second quarter of this year of $690.2 million. That's after paying out $7.9 million in dividends for the first half of this year and allocating $107.8 million to buying back 300,000 shares.

If we assume that the rest of this year will look like the first half of the year did on a year over year basis, we would get net profits of $552 million, adjusted operating cash flow $697.8 million, and EBITDA of $859.5 million. If we use these estimates, and we also use the historical results of the company for the 2024 fiscal year, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. These are multiples that, typically, would indicate either a fair value prospect or one that is just barely in the undervalued category. But seeing as how this is retail, I would certainly push it into the fair value bucket. Relative to other similar firms, however, shares are undeniably expensive. In the chart below, you can see how the business stacks up against two other similar enterprises. Using all three valuation approaches, we can see that it ended up being more expensive than these other enterprises.

Takeaway

Right now, I can understand why some investors might appreciate Dillard's. The company certainly had a positive quarter. Revenue and earnings per share came in higher than expected. But I see this as more a blip on the radar than I do an indication that the business is turning around. Perhaps I will be surprised when new data comes out for the third quarter. But as things stand, I continue to view this as a retailer that is in a permanent state of decline. Even though shares are not ridiculously priced, the fact that management seems more intent on just returning cash to shareholders as opposed to reinventing the business justifies a ‘sell’ rating to me.

data.title: AES Still Offers A Risk-Reward Play
data.description:
I recommend a speculative Buy in The AES Corporation (NYSE:AES) for risk aware investors willing to ride the volatility. There are plenty of concerns around debt load and operational underperformance, with the makings of a value trap in AES. However, I see the merit in a calculated risk position here, given valuations, reasonable margins, secular AI and data center demand, and an optionality of beneficial takeovers.

Valuations and Why They are So Low

The forward PE ratio for AES is currently at ~6x. There has been a close to 30% rally in shares leading to a minor uptick in valuations. Several times AES has touched the $10 mark and rebounded in the past few months. Despite a sizeable technical and speculative rebound, valuations are still visibly lower than the sector median of ~18x.

There are several reasons why AES is valued the way it is. I see the net debt at around $30b, where its market cap is below $10b. The net debt to market cap ratio is obviously a function of share prices. If share prices reach an average sector median range in valuation terms), the ratio will obviously look manageable. I am highlighting the net debt to market cap ratio to highlight how the valuations have factored in the debt load concerns.

The magnitude of debt load concerns is reflected in the net debt to total common equity ratio which works out to be ~8.7x. This is an extremely high leveraged position in my opinion, where my expectations are generally around the 2x-3x mark for utility companies to start talking about financial health.

The elevated debt ratios can only come down in the event of higher retained earnings or equity issuance. Earnings improvement, or a dividend cut could help higher retained earnings. The former is not happening as of now at the levels that can meaningfully change the debt picture. The latter i.e. a dividend cut would be a further blow to a reeling stock, whose case is still supported by a healthy dividend story.

The current levels of net interest expenses at around $1.4b are not an existential threat though they remain concerning. An TTM operating income of $1.7b is just about enough to cover interest expenses and leaves little room for interest expense increases or any operational hiccup. Debt data as of March 2025 shows a majority of debt as fixed coupons which should be dependent on benchmark rate changes. Debt is also shown to be of far out maturities, removing near term refinancing risks. I will await the June 2025 data, but I don't see any overall trend interest serviceability threat from rising rates, apart from a few short term commercial papers.

Free cash flow strain has also been dragging AES for a few years now. The picture is actually improving in the past few quarters, but the company will need a turnaround, not improvement alone to signal financial soundness. Capex strains due to renewables investments and acquisitions could pressure liquidity if the free cash flow conversion does not improve. Stake sales in AES' insurance arm and Ohio show the funding strain to facilitate transition into renewables and keep operations liquid. Dividends are not directly at stake though, with the current yield of ~5.4% corresponding to a ~35% of net income as payout. But the combination of dividends straining earnings retention, high debt levels, and asset sales to fund operations, all put together do not paint a great picture.

Margin Expansion

The thesis so far has explained why valuations are cheap. There are material concerns around AES' financial condition and low room for shocks from here on. The rest of the thesis will focus on why AES is not a Sell, but a "speculative Buy" at this point.

The first important factor is that the dividends are more protected than the lack of free cash flow suggests. The second is that valuations have factored in a lot of the negatives already, leaving little downside room for the shares unless the situation deteriorates. As of now, I see an improving free cash flow trajectory. I also see scope for a revival in net income margins as renewables footprint expands.

In the Q2 2025 earnings call, management has confirmed that AES is on track to deliver 3.2 GW of renewables projects that should support an expected $2.75b Adjusted EBITDA and $2.18 Adjusted EPS at the guidance mid points. As traction in renewables improves, I expect higher margin earnings to show up. AES has also confirmed ~12 GW of PPA backlog that signals strong data center demand and long-term margin visibility.

A Speculative Buy Betting on Turnaround

Although fundamentals scream a value trap without executional proof of any incoming turnaround, I feel the renewables and data center tailwinds are what tilt the thesis to a speculative Buy. Fundamentals are backward looking and valuations have priced in a lot of the negatives. I have bypassed my usual "waiting for a turnaround" approach in the case of AES because the margin improvements look on track, although not seen already. If we wait for the journey to renewables and an eventual margin improvement to be manifested, most of the deep value opportunity we see now would have passed. Hence my calculated risk of a Buy now. I see the renewables improvement as a high probability event and will ride the financial risks till that point is reached.

On the downside, $10 should be a decent psychological support for the stock, as already seen multiple times. I would keep money in hand to average out positions on any contraction towards those levels, if the fundamental story around renewables is still intact (i.e. in simple terms the demand remains strong and AES keeps showing the financial ability to keep going towards that target). Dividends should add to the downside cushion for investors, although that is a smaller piece of the larger question around share price appreciation.

Although speculative, any takeover or acquisition bid could be positive for the share prices as well (depending on the terms of the transaction). Current low valuations make the case for a positive outcome more probable in such a scenario. This remains an optionality.
